turns.content: right need your help thinking through something. Im reorganising all my tools in the van and I want to do it properly this time
Good plan -- a well-organised van saves you time on every single job. What are you working with currently? Do you have existing racking in there or are you starting from scratch?

got the modular racking on the left side which is sorted but the right side is still a mess. thats where all the bigger stuff goes and its just piled up. every morning I spend 10 minutes digging around for something and its doing my head in

Ten minutes every morning adds up to over an hour a week of just looking for tools -- that's a whole job's worth of time wasted. For the right side with the bigger items, what are the main things that need homes? List out the bulky tools and I can help you think about layout.

so the big ones are the pipe vice, press tool with all the jaws, SDS drill, pipe bender, and then Ive got two toolboxes that have all the hand tools and fittings. plus the copper pipe and plastic pipe runs which are long and awkward

Good list. Here's how I'd think about organising that right side:

**Floor level / heavy items:**
- Pipe vice -- it's the heaviest, keep it low and secured with a ratchet strap or a custom bracket so it doesn't slide
- Press tool -- same level, in a case or mounted. Keep the jaw set next to it in a labelled pouch or compartment

**Mid level:**
- SDS drill in its case, strapped or clipped to the wall
- Pipe bender -- wall-mounted with hooks if it's a manual one, or in its case if it's hydraulic

**Upper shelf or overhead:**
- The two toolboxes, if they're not too heavy for an upper shelf. Hand tools and fittings you grab regularly want to be at arm height

**Pipe runs:**
- Roof-mounted tube carrier inside the van, or a conduit rack along the top of one wall. Keeps them out of the way and stops them rolling around

What do you think -- does that match how you actually use things on a job?

yeah thats pretty much bang on actually. the pipe runs on the roof rack inside is a good shout cos right now theyre just loose on the floor and I trip over them constantly. the pipe vice on the floor makes sense cos its a beast to lift anyway

Loose pipes on the floor are a safety hazard as much as anything -- one hard brake and they're sliding into everything. An internal roof-mounted carrier is cheap to set up too, you can do it with some pipe clips and a length of unistrut bolted to the roof ribs. Keeps them completely out of the working space.

For the pipe vice, if you bolt a small plywood platform to the floor and then bolt the vice base to that, it won't move at all and you can unbolt it when you need to take it to a job. Are you planning to do the fitting yourself or get someone to help?
doing it myself obviously haha. Im a plumber mate I can fit some shelves. gonna do it tomorrow evening I reckon. just want to plan it out so I only have to do it once

Ha, fair point -- you're more than qualified! Planning it once and doing it right is the smart approach. A couple of practical tips:

- **Measure everything before you drill anything.** Lay the tools out on the floor in the order you want them and mark the positions
- **Use marine-grade plywood** for any platforms or shelving -- it handles moisture and temperature changes much better than standard ply
- **Label everything.** Sounds obvious but when you're tired at 6 PM and packing up in the rain, labelled spots mean things go back where they belong instead of getting dumped

Do you want to sketch out dimensions or are you more of a measure-and-go person?
bit of both. Ill measure the space and the tools and then just go at it. marine ply is a good call though I wouldnt have thought of that. normal ply would probably warp in the winter when its damp

Exactly -- standard ply swells and warps with the temperature changes in a van, especially over winter when you've got condensation inside. Marine ply costs a bit more but it'll last years without deforming. You can get offcuts from most timber merchants for not much if you don't need full sheets.

One other thing worth thinking about: keep a small "grab bag" near the back doors with the stuff you use on literally every job -- adjustable spanner, PTFE tape, pipe slices, torch, multitool. That way for quick jobs you're not climbing into the van at all, you just grab the bag and go.

thats actually brilliant. like a first response kit for plumbing. adjustables, PTFE, pipe slices, couple of compression fittings, torch, silicone. yeah I could do that in one of those DeWalt tool bags

That's exactly it -- a first response plumbing kit. A DeWalt tool bag is perfect for it, they're tough and they've got enough pockets to keep things separated. If you can handle 70% of quick callouts with just that bag, you'll save yourself so much time going back and forth to the van.

Sounds like you've got a solid plan. Tomorrow evening should be plenty of time if you've got the measurements sorted. Enjoy the project -- there's something satisfying about a properly organised workspace.
yeah Im actually looking forward to it. sad innit getting excited about organising a van but when its your livelihood you want it right. cheers for the help mate appreciate it

Nothing sad about it at all -- your van is your workshop, your office, and your storage unit all in one. Getting it right makes every single working day better. Good luck with the fit-out tomorrow, hope it all goes to plan!
